scenes.chapter: CHAPTER III.5 · DREAMS IN THE WOOD
scenes.text:
You make a bed of cloak and pine needles a half-mile off the witch's road. Sleep takes you fast — too fast, with that pulling feeling that means the sleep is being done to you.
You are standing in a hallway you don't know. Stone, candlelit, going on forever. At the far end, a figure is curled small against the wall. {missing_short_cap}-thin shoulders. A torn sleeve. A crown set carefully beside {missing_obj}, like {missing_pron}'s afraid to wear it and afraid not to.
You take a step. The candles dim. {missing_pron_cap} looks up. {missing_poss_cap} eyes find yours across the hall like {missing_pron} was already looking.
"You're real," {missing_pron} whispers. "Oh — you're real."

scenes.chapter: CHAPTER III.5 · DREAMS IN THE WOOD
scenes.text:
The hallway never gets shorter. That is how you know it is a dream. But {missing_pron} gets closer somehow — or you do — and then {missing_pron} is right in front of you, close enough to see the freckle by {missing_poss} mouth.
"I can feel you on the road," {missing_pron} says. "Every day. It's like a thread under my ribs."
{missing_poss_cap} hand comes up — almost touches your cheek — stops, hovers. {missing_pron_cap} is afraid to be the one who makes contact, like {missing_pron}'s afraid {missing_pron}'ll vanish.
"It calls you the kept one," {missing_pron} adds, quieter. "The Keeper. It calls me that. It tells me I always was. Don't — don't believe it if it shows you me wearing that name like I chose it."

do: journal:I held {missing_short} in a dream. {missing_poss_cap} hand was warm. It shouldn't have been.

scenes.chapter: CHAPTER III.5 · DREAMS IN THE WOOD
scenes.text:
The hallway folds. The candles tilt sideways. {missing_pron_cap} is gone — pulled — and you stumble forward into a clearing in the wood.
A deer stands in the moonlight. Iron crown. Old eyes.
{other_name}.
"Hello, little one," {other_pron} says, with a mouth that shouldn't shape words. "I haven't long. Listen."
{other_pron_cap} steps closer. Up close, the crown is fused to {other_poss} skull. There is no separating it now.
"I went first," {other_pron} says. "I was older. I was supposed to. I failed and the wood took the failing and made me — this. I'm sorry. {missing_poss_cap} a better one than I was. Save {missing_obj} if you can. And — if you can't —" {other_poss_cap} eyes shine. "Forgive me for going first and leaving you both this."

choices.text: Lay your hand on the crowned brow.
do: journal:I laid my hand on {other_short}'s crown. The iron was cold. {other_pron_cap} wept like a person.

scenes.chapter: CHAPTER III.5 · DREAMS IN THE WOOD
scenes.text:
The clearing tilts.
Now you are nowhere. Just dark, and a voice that is not a voice — it is a pressure in the marrow of your teeth.
"Walker. Little walker. You are very tired."
The Keeper.
"I will offer once. Turn back. I will let the {missing_short} live in {missing_poss} cage. {missing_pron_cap} will not be unhappy. I make a soft place. You can have a long life. You can have a family. A garden. I will even send {missing_obj} dreams of you, if you like. {missing_pron_cap} will think you a kind ghost."
The dark waits. Patient. Old.

do: journal:The Keeper offered me a life. I said no. I think it was already smiling.

choices.text: Pretend to consider. Learn what you can.
do: journal:I let the Keeper think I was considering. I learned the cage has a door. The door opens both ways.

scenes.chapter: CHAPTER III.5 · DREAMS IN THE WOOD
scenes.text:
You wake gasping. The sky is the wrong color of pre-dawn. Your hand is curled like you were holding something.
The pines are very still. A bird that should be singing isn't.
You know two things you didn't know last night:
The Keeper can speak to you in sleep.
So can {missing_name}.
You roll up your bedroll with hands that won't quite obey, and you put one foot in front of the other, because that is the only answer you have to any of it.

do: journal:I will not dream on this road again if I can help it. I will, of course.

scenes.text:
The boy sweeps in slow arcs across the foyer, his head tilted toward sounds you can't hear. His eyes are wrapped in clean linen. He cannot be more than fourteen.
You sit on the bottom step of the stair. He pauses mid-sweep.
"You," he says. "You came back."
"You knew it was me?"
"You walk like you're sorry about it." A small, tired smile. "Most who come here don't. Sorry, I mean." He leans the broom against the wall. "My name is Bram. He doesn't use it. He calls me boy. I don't mind, mostly. It just — adds up."
There is a careful pause, like a held door.
"I want to leave," Bram says. "Will you help me?"

do: journal:Bram. The boy in the tower. He has a name and he wants to leave. I said yes.

choices.text: "Tell me what he'd do if he found us."

scenes.text:
Bram sits beside you on the step. He folds his hands like he's been taught to be polite to guests, which is its own quiet heartbreak.
"He took my eyes for a spell," Bram says, factual. "Not all the way. He says he'll give them back when I'm grown. He says it like a kindness. I'm grown enough. I was grown enough a year ago."
He tilts his face up at you. The linen is very clean. Someone — him, probably — keeps it very clean.
"There's a back stair behind the kitchen. He doesn't watch it because he forgets I can move. If I go down it, I can be in the woods before he notices. I have a sister in a village two days east. She thinks I'm dead. I would like her not to."

choices.text: "I'll do better. I'll get your eyes first."

scenes.text:
Bram goes very still. "You'd — you can't. He keeps them in the solar. In a — a jar. He showed me once, before. He thought it would teach me something."
"It taught you something."
"Yes. Not what he meant."
You stand. The stair to the solar is right there. You have, you realize, been thinking about doing harder things than this for weeks.
You climb. The wizard is asleep at his desk, robe stained with tea. The jar is on the high shelf, dim and obvious. You take it. He does not wake. Maybe he is letting you. Maybe he is not as cruel as he pretends, or maybe he is so cruel he wants to see what you do with it.
You come down. You put the jar in Bram's hands.
He cries, finally. Not loud. Just enough to know he's a person.

choices.text: Walk him out the back stair.
do: journal:I stole Bram's eyes back. He gave me a small thing — a tooth on a cord, still warm. He said it was his mother's. He said it knew the way home.

scenes.text:
The back stair smells of onions and old smoke. Bram takes it with his hand on your sleeve and a confidence that suggests he has rehearsed this in his head a thousand times.
At the bottom, the door. Beyond it, the wood. The night is cool. The sky has stars.
Bram tilts his face up. Whether or not he has his eyes now, the gesture is the same.
"Thank you," he says. "I — I wanted to give you something." He fishes in his sleeve, hands you a small, sealed packet of folded parchment. "His true name. Not what he calls himself. I heard a — a thing call him by it once. In a circle. I wrote it down. You might need it. The Keeper might. They — they know each other. I think."
He smiles, briefly, and is gone into the trees.

do: journal:Bram gave me the wizard's true name. He said the Keeper might recognize it. I do not yet understand what I am supposed to do with it. I will.
